subject: Physics
q: A particle is moving with constant acceleration a. Following graph shows v2 versus x (displacement) plot. The acceleration of the particle is _________ m s-2.
question: A particle is moving with constant acceleration a. Following graph shows v2 versus x (displacement) plot. The acceleration of the particle is _________ m s-2.
solution:
The equation of the straight line, y=mx+c, from the above graph we get the equation, v2=2x+20, now differentiate it with respect to x, 
⇒2vdvdx=2⇒a=22=1 m s-2.

subject: Physics
q: A parachutist after bailing out falls $50 \mathrm{~m}$ without friction. When parachute opens, it decelerates at $2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$. He reaches the ground with a speed of $3 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. At what height, did he bail out?
question: A parachutist after bailing out falls $50 \mathrm{~m}$ without friction. When parachute opens, it decelerates at $2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$. He reaches the ground with a speed of $3 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. At what height, did he bail out?
solution:
$s=50+\left(\frac{3^2-(2 \times 10 \times 50)}{2(-2)}\right)$
$=293 \mathrm{~m}$.

q: If the velocity of a body related to displacement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi><mo>=</mo><msqrt><mn>5000</mn><mo>+</mo><mn>24</mn><mi>x</mi></msqrt><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo></math> then the acceleration of the body is _________ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math>
question: If the velocity of a body related to displacement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi><mo>=</mo><msqrt><mn>5000</mn><mo>+</mo><mn>24</mn><mi>x</mi></msqrt><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo></math> then the acceleration of the body is _________ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
We know that the rate of change of linear velocity is called linear acceleration, a=dvdt=dv×dxdt×dx=vdvdx

⇒a=5000+24x×125000+24x×24=12 m s-2.

subject: Physics
q: A ball is released from a height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> be the time required to complete first half and second half of the distance respectively. Then, choose the correct relation between <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>.
question: A ball is released from a height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> be the time required to complete first half and second half of the distance respectively. Then, choose the correct relation between <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>.
solution:
As the body is dropped from a certain height h, its initial velocity will be zero.

For first half, h1=h2.

Using second equation of motion, s=ut+12at2

We have, h2=12gt12.

Time taken in travel first half of the height, t1=hg    ...1.

For total height h, using again second equation of motion,

h=12gt1+t22⇒t1+t2=2hg    ...2

From equation 1 and 2, 

⇒12=t1t1+t2

⇒1+t2t1=2

⇒t1t2=12-1

Thus, the relation is t2=2-1t1.

subject: Physics
q: Two cars $A$ and $B$ are moving in the same direction along a straight line with speeds $100$ km/h and $80$ km/h, respectively such that car $A$ is moving ahead of car $B$. A person in car $B$ throws a stone with a speed $v$ so that it hits the car $A$ with a speed of $5$ m/s. The value of $v$ is ______ km/h.
question: Two cars $A$ and $B$ are moving in the same direction along a straight line with speeds $100$ km/h and $80$ km/h, respectively such that car $A$ is moving ahead of car $B$. A person in car $B$ throws a stone with a speed $v$ so that it hits the car $A$ with a speed of $5$ m/s. The value of $v$ is ______ km/h.
solution:
Velocity of car A, $v_A = 100$ km/h

Velocity of car B, $v_B = 80$ km/h

Velocity of the stone relative to car B is $v$. The velocity of the stone relative to the ground is $v_S = v_B + v = 80 + v$.

The velocity of the stone relative to car A is $v_{S/A} = v_S - v_A = (80 + v) - 100 = v - 20$.

The stone hits car A with a relative speed of $5$ m/s. Converting this speed to km/h:

$v_{S/A} = 5 \times \dfrac{18}{5} = 18$ km/h

Equating the relative speeds:

$v - 20 = 18 \Rightarrow v = 38$ km/h

Answer: $38$

q: A body is thrown vertically upwards. Which one of the following graphs correctly represents the velocity<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>v</mi></mfenced></math> vs time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>t</mi></mfenced></math>?
question: A body is thrown vertically upwards. Which one of the following graphs correctly represents the velocity<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>v</mi></mfenced></math> vs time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>t</mi></mfenced></math>?
solution:
v=u-gt

y=c+mx

m=-g = slope

c=+u

Velocity becomes negative after some time and the negative slope is constant.

subject: Physics
q: A passenger sitting in a train A moving at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>90</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> observes another train B moving in the opposite direction for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. If the velocity of the train B is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>54</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, then length of train B is:
question: A passenger sitting in a train A moving at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>90</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> observes another train B moving in the opposite direction for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. If the velocity of the train B is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>54</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, then length of train B is:
solution:
Let the velocity of A be vA and the velocity of B be vB.

The relative velocity is

v→BA =v→B-v→A⇒vBA= 54-(-90)=144 km h-1

⇒vBA= 40 m s-1

Time=lengthvBA 

⇒length= 40 m s-1 × 8 s=320 m

q: The position vector of a particle changes with time according to the relation <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>15</mn><msup><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>4</mn><mo>-</mo><mn>20</mn><msup><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>.</mo></math> What is the magnitude of the acceleration at <math><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>?</mo></math>
question: The position vector of a particle changes with time according to the relation <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>15</mn><msup><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>4</mn><mo>-</mo><mn>20</mn><msup><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>.</mo></math> What is the magnitude of the acceleration at <math><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>?</mo></math>
solution:
r→=15t2i^+4-20t2j^
v→=30 ti^-40t j^
a→=30 i^-40j^
At t=1 ,
a→=30 i^-40j^
a→=50

subject: Physics
q: A ball is thrown vertically up (taken as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>+</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> ) from the ground. The correct momentum-height <math><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mo>)</mo></math> diagram is:
question: A ball is thrown vertically up (taken as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>+</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> ) from the ground. The correct momentum-height <math><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mo>)</mo></math> diagram is:
solution:
If the ball was thrown with initial velocity u , then its velocity at height 'h' can be written as.
v=u2-2gh
Its linear momentum, p=mv=mu2-2gh
Or  p2=m2u2-2m2gh
The graph between p & h will be a parabola.
In motion when particle was going up, its velocity is positive and decreasing with height and so is the linear momentum. When it reaches the maximum height, the velocity and linear momentum will be zero. Again, it will start to fall back, the velocity and linear momentum will increase but will have negative value.

subject: Physics
q: A clock has $75 \mathrm{~cm}, 60 \mathrm{~cm}$ long second hand and minute hand respectively. In 30 minutes duration the tip of second hand will travel $x$ distance more than the tip of minute hand. The value of $x$ in meter is nearly (Take $\pi=3.14$ ) :
question: A clock has $75 \mathrm{~cm}, 60 \mathrm{~cm}$ long second hand and minute hand respectively. In 30 minutes duration the tip of second hand will travel $x$ distance more than the tip of minute hand. The value of $x$ in meter is nearly (Take $\pi=3.14$ ) :
solution: $\begin{aligned} \mathrm{x}_{\min }= & \pi \times \mathrm{r}_{\min } \\ = & \pi \times \frac{60}{100} \mathrm{~m} . \\ \mathrm{x}_{\text {second }} & =30 \times 2 \pi \times \mathrm{r}_{\text {second }} \\ & =30 \times 2 \pi \times \frac{75}{100} \\ \mathrm{x}= & \mathrm{x}_{\text {second }}-\mathrm{x}_{\min } \\ = & 139.4 \mathrm{~m}\end{aligned}$

In the last <math><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> before hitting the ground, it covers a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>19</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Acceleration due to gravity (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> ) near the surface on that planet is______

subject: Physics
q: A bus moving along a straight highway with speed of $72 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$ is brought to halt within $4 s$ after applying the brakes. The distance travelled by the bus during this time (Assume the retardation is uniform) is _____m.
question: A bus moving along a straight highway with speed of $72 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$ is brought to halt within $4 s$ after applying the brakes. The distance travelled by the bus during this time (Assume the retardation is uniform) is _____m.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { Initial velocity }=u=72 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}=20 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} \\ & \mathrm{v}=\mathrm{u}+\text { at } \\ & \Rightarrow 0=20+\mathrm{a} \times 4 \\ & \mathrm{a}=-5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2 \\ & \mathrm{v}^2-\mathrm{u}^2=2 \text { as } \\ & \Rightarrow 0^2-20^2=2(-5) . \mathrm{s} \\ & \mathrm{s}=40 \mathrm{~m}\end{aligned}$

The retardation of the motion is: (When <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi></math> stands for velocity)
solution:
t=mx2+nx

1v=dtdx=2mx+n

V=12mx+n

dvdt=-2m(2mx+n)2dxdt

a=-(2m)v3

subject: Physics
q:
A paratrooper jumps from an aeroplane and opens a parachute after 2 s of free fall and starts deaccelerating with $3 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$. At 10 m height from ground, while descending with the help of parachute, the speed of paratrooper is $5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. The initial height of the airplane is $\_\_\_\_$ m.
$\left(\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}\right)$
question:
A paratrooper jumps from an aeroplane and opens a parachute after 2 s of free fall and starts deaccelerating with $3 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$. At 10 m height from ground, while descending with the help of parachute, the speed of paratrooper is $5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. The initial height of the airplane is $\_\_\_\_$ m.
$\left(\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}\right)$
solution:
During the first $2\text{ s}$ of free fall, the paratrooper starts from rest ($u = 0$).
Velocity after $2\text{ s}$ is $v_1 = u + gt = 0 + 10 \times 2 = 20\text{ m/s}$.
Distance covered during free fall is $h_1 = \frac{1}{2}gt^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 10 \times 2^2 = 20\text{ m}$.
After opening the parachute, the paratrooper decelerates at $a = -3\text{ m/s}^2$.
At a height of $10\text{ m}$ from the ground, the final velocity is $v_2 = 5\text{ m/s}$.
Let $h_2$ be the distance covered during deceleration until reaching $10\text{ m}$ height.
Using $v_2^2 = v_1^2 + 2ah_2$:
$5^2 = 20^2 + 2(-3)h_2$
$25 = 400 - 6h_2$
$6h_2 = 375$
$h_2 = 62.5\text{ m}$.
The total height of the airplane is the sum of the free fall distance, the deceleration distance, and the remaining height from the ground.
$H = h_1 + h_2 + 10 = 20 + 62.5 + 10 = 92.5\text{ m}$.

subject: Physics
q:
A NCC parade is going at a uniform speed of 9 km h-1 under a mango tree on which a monkey is sitting at a height of 19.6 m. At any particular instant, the monkey drops a mango. A cadet will receive the mango whose distance from the tree at time of drop is :
(Given g=9.8 m s-2)
question:
A NCC parade is going at a uniform speed of 9 km h-1 under a mango tree on which a monkey is sitting at a height of 19.6 m. At any particular instant, the monkey drops a mango. A cadet will receive the mango whose distance from the tree at time of drop is :
(Given g=9.8 m s-2)
solution:
If a cadet receives the mango, then the time taken by the mango to fall and the cadet to reach there should be equal.

Time taken by mango to fall t=2hg.

⇒t=2×19.69.8=2 s

Therefore, the distance of the cadet from the tree will be, d=vt.

⇒d=9×518×2=5 m

subject: Physics
q: For a train engine moving with speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>ms</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, the driver must apply brakes at a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>500</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> before the station for the train to come to rest at the station. If the brakes were applied at half of this distance, the train engine would cross the station with speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mi>x</mi></msqrt><mo> </mo><msup><mi>ms</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is ______. (Assuming same retardation is produced by brakes)
question: For a train engine moving with speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>ms</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, the driver must apply brakes at a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>500</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> before the station for the train to come to rest at the station. If the brakes were applied at half of this distance, the train engine would cross the station with speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mi>x</mi></msqrt><mo> </mo><msup><mi>ms</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is ______. (Assuming same retardation is produced by brakes)
solution:
Given that, the initial velocity u=20 m/s, the distance of the station S1=500 m, and the final velocity v=0.

The relation between the distance travelled S by the train with its initial velocity u and its final velocity v is given by

v2= u2-2aS........................(1)

where, a indicates the retardation of the train.

Substitute the values corresponding to the initial condition into equation (1) and solve to calculate the retardation of the train.

0=(20)2-2×a×500a=0.4 m/s2

For the second scenario, the initial velocity u=20 m/s and the distance travelled by the train S2=250 m.

Use these values along with the value of the retardation into equation (1) and solve to calculate the final velocity of the train.

v2=(20)2-2×0.4×250= 200v= 200..........................(2)

Comparing the value of the final velocity obtained in equation (2) with the given expression, we get x= 200.

subject: Physics
q: From the top of a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>64</mn></math> metres high tower, a stone is thrown upwards vertically with the velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>48</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>/</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The greatest height (in metres) attained by the stone, assuming the value of the gravitational acceleration <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>32</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>/</mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>, is:
question: From the top of a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>64</mn></math> metres high tower, a stone is thrown upwards vertically with the velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>48</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>/</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The greatest height (in metres) attained by the stone, assuming the value of the gravitational acceleration <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>32</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>/</mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>, is:
solution:
We know that v2=u2+2gs.

At greatest height v=0

u=48 m/sec

g=-32 m/sec2

S=48×482.32=36 m

Total distance =64 m+36 m=100 m

=100 m

subject: Physics
q: A body falling under gravity covers two points A and B separated by 80 m in 2 s. The distance of upper point A from the starting point is _____m. Use g=10 m s-2
question: A body falling under gravity covers two points A and B separated by 80 m in 2 s. The distance of upper point A from the starting point is _____m. Use g=10 m s-2
solution:
From A→B, using the equation of motion, we have

-80=-v1t-12×10t2⇒-80=-2v1-12×10×22⇒-80=-2v1-20⇒-60=-2v1⇒v1=30 m s-1

From O to A, it follows that

v2=u2+2gS⇒302=0+2×(-10)(-S)⇒900=20 S⇒S=45 m

subject: Physics
q: A body of mass 2 kg moving with velocity of $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_{\mathrm{in}}=3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{j}} \mathrm{ms}^{-1}$ enters into a constant force field of 6 N directed along positive z -axis. If the body remains in the field for a period of $\frac{5}{3}$ seconds, then velocity of the body when it emerges from force field is
question: A body of mass 2 kg moving with velocity of $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_{\mathrm{in}}=3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{j}} \mathrm{ms}^{-1}$ enters into a constant force field of 6 N directed along positive z -axis. If the body remains in the field for a period of $\frac{5}{3}$ seconds, then velocity of the body when it emerges from force field is
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}=\frac{\mathrm{B}}{2} \hat{\mathrm{k}}=3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}, \mathrm{t}=\frac{5}{3} \mathrm{~s} \\ & \overrightarrow{\mathrm{u}}=3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{j}} \\ & \overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{u}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \mathrm{t}=3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+5 \hat{\mathrm{k}}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A particle is moving in a straight line such that its velocity is increasing at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> per meter. The acceleration of the particle is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> at a point where its velocity is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>.
question: A particle is moving in a straight line such that its velocity is increasing at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> per meter. The acceleration of the particle is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> at a point where its velocity is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>.
solution:
According to the question, velocity is increasing at 5 m s-1. Therefore, dvds=5 s-1. 

Now acceleration a=dvdt=vdvds

⇒a=vdvds=20×5=100 m s-2

subject: Physics
q: A bullet loses <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>n</mi></mfrac></mfenced><mi>th</mi></msup></math> of its velocity passing through one plank. Considering uniform retardation, the number of such planks that are required to stop the bullet can be:
question: A bullet loses <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>n</mi></mfrac></mfenced><mi>th</mi></msup></math> of its velocity passing through one plank. Considering uniform retardation, the number of such planks that are required to stop the bullet can be:
solution:
Let t be the thickness of the plank

υ01-1n2=υ02-2at

∴ 2at=υ02-υ021-1n2

If m number of planks are required to stop a bullet, then

0=υ02-2atm

∴ m=υ022at

=υ02υ021-1-1n2

=n2n2-n-12=n22n-1

subject: Physics
q: Consider a rubber ball freely falling from a height $h=4.9 \mathrm{~m}$ onto a horizontal elastic plate. Assume that the duration of collision is negligible and the collision with the plate is totally elastic. Then the velocity as a function of time the height as function of time will be
question: Consider a rubber ball freely falling from a height $h=4.9 \mathrm{~m}$ onto a horizontal elastic plate. Assume that the duration of collision is negligible and the collision with the plate is totally elastic. Then the velocity as a function of time the height as function of time will be
solution: $\mathrm{h}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{gt}^2, \quad \mathrm{v}=-\mathrm{gt}$ and after the collision, $\mathrm{v}=\mathrm{gt}$. (parabolic) (straight line) Collision is perfectly elastic then ball reaches to same height again and again with same velocity.

subject: Physics
q: A particle moves in a straight line so that its displacement $x$ at any time $t$ is given by $x^2=1+t^2$. Its acceleration at any time $\mathrm{t}$ is $x^{-\mathrm{n}}$ where $\mathrm{n}=$ ___________
question: A particle moves in a straight line so that its displacement $x$ at any time $t$ is given by $x^2=1+t^2$. Its acceleration at any time $\mathrm{t}$ is $x^{-\mathrm{n}}$ where $\mathrm{n}=$ ___________
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{x}^2=1+\mathrm{t}^2 \\ & 2 \mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{dt}}=2 \mathrm{t} \\ & \mathrm{xv}=\mathrm{t} \\ & \mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dt}}+\mathrm{v} \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{dt}}=1 \\ & \mathrm{x} \cdot \mathrm{a}+\mathrm{v}^2=1 \\ & \mathrm{a}=\frac{1-\mathrm{v}^2}{\mathrm{x}}=\frac{1-\mathrm{t}^2 / \mathrm{x}^2}{\mathrm{x}} \\ & \mathrm{a}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}^3}=\mathrm{x}^{-3}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A person climbs up a stalled escalator in $60 \mathrm{~s}$. If standing on the same but escalator running with constant velocity he takes $40 \mathrm{~s}$. How much time is taken by the person to walk up the moving escalator?
question: A person climbs up a stalled escalator in $60 \mathrm{~s}$. If standing on the same but escalator running with constant velocity he takes $40 \mathrm{~s}$. How much time is taken by the person to walk up the moving escalator?
solution:
Person's speed walking only is $\frac{1}{60} \frac{\text { "escalator" }}{\text { second }}$
Standing the escalator without walking the
speed is $\frac{1}{40} \frac{\text { "escalator" }}{\text { second }}$
Walking with the escalator going, the speed add.
So, the person's speed is $\frac{1}{60}+\frac{1}{40}=\frac{15}{120}$
$\frac{\text { "escalator" }}{\text { second }}$
So, the time to go up the escalator ("seconds per escalator") is

$$
t=\frac{120}{5}=24 \text { second }
$$

subject: Physics
q:
An object of mass m is being moved with a constant velocity under the action of an applied force of 2 N along a frictionless surface with following surface profile.

The correct applied force vs distance graph will be :
question:
An object of mass m is being moved with a constant velocity under the action of an applied force of 2 N along a frictionless surface with following surface profile.

The correct applied force vs distance graph will be :
solution:
When the block will be moving up the incline, the external force will be up the incline and will be balancing the down the incline component of gravity.

When the block will be moving down the incline, the external force will be again up the incline and will be balancing the down the incline component of gravity. But in this case the external force will be on the back side of the block.

F=mgsinθ=2 N
